title: US Strikes Hit Iranian Nuclear Site as IRGC Torpedoes Hormuz Tankers, Farnborough Opens Under War Shadow
subhead: The ninth consecutive night of US strikes on Iran included a hit on a nuclear facility under construction, while IRGC attacks on oil tankers effectively shuttered the Strait of Hormuz. The Farnborough Air Show opened with Boeing, Rafael, and European sovereignty debates dominating proceedings.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iranian Nuclear Construction Site as Campaign Enters Ninth Night
sections.body:
The US military said it completed its ninth consecutive night of strikes against Iran, targeting command centres, air defence sites, coastal surveillance, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and communications networks. CENTCOM said the strikes further diminished 'Iran's ability to attack commercial vessels and civilian mariners transiting the Strait of Hormuz.'

In a significant escalation, Iran's atomic energy agency confirmed that US strikes hit the construction site of a planned nuclear power plant at Darkhovin in south-western Iran, according to state television. The International Atomic Energy Agency said the site was in 'very early stages of construction' and contained no nuclear material at the time of the IAEA's last visit. Satellite imagery from Planet Labs showed only earth clearing at the site as of July 9.

President Trump said of the strikes: 'We hit them very hard again tonight... and we did that in honour [of the slain soldiers].' Trump has signalled plans to hit more Iranian infrastructure, including bridges, electrical power plants, and distribution systems. Seventeen US service members have been killed since the war began. A service member was also killed in northern Iraq during the controlled detonation of a downed Iranian drone on Saturday, CENTCOM reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The strike on a nuclear construction site — even one without material — marks a qualitative escalation that could reshape proliferation norms and Iran's calculus about restarting its nuclear programme. The expanding target set to civilian infrastructure (bridges, power plants) signals a shift from military degradation toward coercive economic targeting.

sections.heading: IRGC Attacks Oil Tankers in Hormuz, Strait Traffic Drops to 3-8 Transits Per Day
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps claimed responsibility for attacks on two oil tankers that exploded and were immobilised while attempting to transit a southern route through the Strait of Hormuz, which the US military had been encouraging ships to use. The IRGC declared the waterway would 'not be safe for the transit of petrochemical products, nor even a single drop of oil and gas' as long as US 'aggression' continues, and warned the US military to prepare for a 'punitive operation.'

A ship also caught fire near the coast of Oman, the UK Maritime Trade Operations reported, though the cause was unclear. The IRGC statement gave no details on the vessels' names, flags, crews, or casualties. A US Navy maritime organisation said traffic through the strait had effectively stalled, with only eight transits on Saturday and three on Friday, compared to a pre-war daily average of nearly 140.

The US has reimposed a naval blockade on Iranian ports, saying it has redirected six ships and disabled one since the blockade began. Benchmark Brent crude rose above $90 a barrel, fuelling a global energy crisis sparked by the conflict.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz shutdown is now near-total, with the IRGC explicitly targeting the alternate route the US Navy was promoting. The 95%+ drop in tanker traffic from 140 to 3-8 daily transits demonstrates that military assurances are not translating into commercial confidence. This has direct implications for global energy supply chains, insurance markets, and defence procurement for maritime domain awareness.

sections.heading: US Deploys Additional F-16s and F-35s to Middle East
sections.body:
The New York Times reported that F-16 fighter jets from Germany and F-35 stealth fighters from Britain were being deployed to the Middle East, along with additional aerial refueling aircraft. An Israeli military official said the US had decided to place some additional aerial refueling aircraft at Israeli Air Force bases.

The redeployments were ordered before an Iranian attack on Friday that killed two Army soldiers in Jordan and left another service member missing, with unidentified remains now being examined. The US has since launched retaliatory strikes against the IRGC. Iran fired missiles toward Jordan in response, which Jordan's military said it shot down without casualties. Kuwait reported a second attack on a power and desalination plant in two days — roughly 90% of Kuwait's drinking water comes from desalination.
sections.whyItMatters: The deployment of additional F-35s and F-16s from European bases signals both an escalation in strike capability and a strain on global USAF posture. Stationing refuelers in Israel is a logistical decision with significant operational implications for sustained air campaign reach. Every aircraft moved to the Middle East reduces availability in Europe and the Pacific.

sections.heading: Farnborough Air Show Opens With Sovereignty and Cost Overruns in Focus
sections.body:
The 2026 Farnborough International Airshow opened under the shadow of the US-Iran conflict and deepening European concern about strategic autonomy. Boeing Defense, Space & Security CEO Steve Parker said the company remains on track to deliver two new Air Force One jets in 2028, but acknowledged additional cost growth on a program already more than $5 billion — up from the original $3.9 billion contract. The program is four years behind schedule.

Boeing is also using Farnborough to pitch its T-7A Red Hawk trainer to the UK, with BAE Systems performing final assembly in Britain — a notable shift for Boeing, which recently lost the NATO AWACS competition to Saab and saw Italy cancel its KC-46 tanker order. Parker said Boeing has been 'listening a little bit differently' as European allies question US reliability. The T-7 is also being pitched with Rheinmetall in Germany for the MQ-28 drone collaboration.

Rafael Advanced Defense Systems arrived at Farnborough with a combat-tested portfolio, showcasing SPICE 250 precision-guided munitions, ROCKS long-range missiles, and the ICE BREAKER fifth-generation cruise missile. The company highlighted Iron Beam laser interception, now operational alongside Iron Dome, and the SPYDER air defence system recently selected by Romania in Rafael's largest-ever defence contract. Germany has approved procurement of 90 LITENING 5 targeting pods for its Eurofighter fleet. Rafael CEO Yoav Tourgeman said 'proven performance and reliable delivery are now the baseline requirements.'
sections.whyItMatters: Farnborough is the first major air show since Europe's rearmament debate shifted from aspiration to procurement. Boeing's pivot to local assembly for the UK and Germany — after losing key competitions — reflects how the US reliability question is reshaping even American contractors' European strategies. Rafael's combat-proven pitch, and Germany's LITENING buy, show that combat validation from Ukraine and the Middle East is now the primary selling point.

sections.heading: Ukraine-EU Sign $1.1 Billion Drone Deal as Kyiv Targets 20 Million Units Per Year
sections.body:
Ukraine and the European Union signed a $1.1 billion defence cooperation agreement known as the 'Drone Deal,' placing drone production at the centre of Kyiv's long-term military planning. President Zelensky claimed Ukraine is already producing 10 million drones annually and aims to double that to 20 million, saying: 'I remember when I first publicly announced the state's plan: 1 million drones per year. There was a lot of skepticism everywhere... We make 10 million drones a year. Ten, and it will be 20.'

Ukraine's National Security and Defence Council Secretary Rustem Umerov said six separate agreements have been completed under the Drone Deal framework, with more than 20 countries working toward joining the same cooperation model. Defence Minister Fedorov claimed Ukrainian forces struck more than 800,000 verified Russian targets using drones in 2026, contributing to approximately 167,000 Russian troops killed or seriously wounded — figures that cannot be independently verified.

Ukrainian officials have described ambitions to eventually exceed 30 million units annually, surpassing the combined output of China, Russia, and the US.
sections.whyItMatters: If Ukraine's production figures are even partially accurate, it has become the world's largest drone manufacturer by volume. The EU's $1.1 billion Drone Deal creates a formal industrial partnership that could sustain Ukraine's drone ecosystem even if US support fluctuates. The 20-country framework also shows how Ukraine's drone warfare experience is being institutionalised as an exportable model for allied defence planning.

sections.heading: Hanwha Philly Shipyard Wins Golden Dome Missile Range Vessel Contract
sections.body:
Hanwha Philly Shipyard was awarded the contract to build missile range instrumentation vessels (MRIV) for the US as part of the Golden Dome programme. The announcement came at a ceremony where Trump administration officials and Rep. Mary Gay Scanlon christened a new training vessel at the yard.

The contract is a notable win for the South Korean-owned shipyard and reflects the broader expansion of US missile defence infrastructure. Golden Dome is the US effort to build a layered homeland missile defence shield, and the MRIV programme provides the sea-based instrumentation and tracking vessels needed to support testing and operations.
sections.whyItMatters: A Korean-owned yard building missile defence instrumentation vessels for the US illustrates the deepening defence industrial integration between Washington and Seoul. The Golden Dome programme's growing procurement pipeline — from interceptors to range instrumentation — signals that homeland missile defence is shifting from concept to hardware, with implications for shipbuilding capacity and the broader industrial base.

sections.heading: The Economist Highlights Europe's Growing Long-Range Strike Gap
sections.body:
The Economist published a detailed analysis of Europe's 'dangerous missile gap,' noting that European armed forces face a growing inability to hit targets at long range. As Russia continues to deploy long-range standoff weapons, European nations have been slow to develop and procure equivalent deep-strike capabilities, with the exception of limited Storm Shadow/SCALP-EG inventories and France's newer ASN4G programme in early development.

The analysis comes as the US-Iran conflict has further strained US munitions stockpiles, reducing the availability of American-made Tomahawk cruise missiles and other precision-guided munitions that European allies might need to purchase. USAF Chief of Staff Gen. Ken Wilsbach also spoke to Breaking Defense about lessons from the Iran campaign, T-7 Red Hawk progress, and ongoing F-35 readiness challenges — a topic covered extensively in previous briefs.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's missile gap was theoretical when the main threat scenario was a Russia contingency in 3-5 years. The US-Iran campaign is consuming the very munitions — Tomahawks, precision-guided bombs — that European nations would need to purchase to close that gap. Every missile fired at an Iranian target is one that cannot be sold to or stored by a European ally. The Economist's analysis adds institutional weight to what procurement data has been showing for months.

bottomLine: The US-Iran conflict crossed a significant threshold this weekend: a nuclear construction site was struck, IRGC attacks on oil tankers have nearly shuttered the Strait of Hormuz (down from 140 daily transits to single digits), and additional F-35s and F-16s are deploying from Europe to the Middle East. For the defence industry, the ninth night of strikes confirms that munitions consumption will remain extraordinary — straining stockpiles that European allies also depend on. At Farnborough, Boeing's local-assembly pitch for the T-7 and Rafael's combat-proven systems showcase illustrate how the reliability question is reshaping procurement strategies. Meanwhile, Ukraine's claim of 10 million drones per year, backed by a $1.1 billion EU deal, signals that drone warfare is moving from battlefield expedient to industrial-scale sovereign capability.
sources: Al Jazeera, New York Times, ABC Australia, Breaking Defense, Reuters, Israel Defense, Aviation Week, Seeking Alpha, Bisnow, The Economist, BBC, Yahoo News/Interfax, MSN, Philadelphia Weekly, Motley Fool
